english: 1. formerly: a Mongol, hor jin-gin-kan (hor jin gin kan) the Mongol Djingiskhan; hor-sér (hor ser) Shara Sharaighol n. of a Mongol tribe Glr.: hór-yul (hor yul) Mongolia. - 2. at present: in C. the people living near the Tengri-nor (; nam-mtso (nam mtso)); in W. the Turks; hór-zla (hor zla) a Turkish month. Note. Cs. has only the second of these significations, Sch. only the first (the latter using Cs.'s examples and changing all the Turks into Mongols!) The suppositions of Latham seem still less consistent with the real state of the case.
Sch.: 'deficiencies, gaps: separation' (?).
Sch.: 'confiscation, hór-_dra bábs-pa (hor 'dra babs pa) to confiscate' (?).
wood-grouse or cock of the wood Sch., hór-pa dkár-po (hor pa dkar po) a species of hawk Sch. (?).
W. soft, loose, light, as the soil in spring, *hol táṅ-če* (hol tang ce), hól-te bór-če* (hol te bor ce) to break up, to loosen (the soil).
1. vb., to require more and more, to covet incessantly Ma., W. - 2. sbst., adj. hardness, hard Cs.

1. alone, cf. raṅ (rang), *raṅ-sráṅ-la yoṅ soṅ* (rang srang la yong song) I came alone C., po-hráṅ (pho hrang), mo-hráṅ (mo hrang) single man and woman, = po-ráṅ (pho rang) etc. C. - 2. with dmar (dmar) preceding, naked C.

1. to thrust, to push violently, to stem firmly, *káṅ-pa sig-pa-la* (rkang pa sig pa la) W. to stem the foot against the wall - 2. to scratch, sgó-la p'yag-hrád-pa gu mdzád-pa (sgo la phyag hrad pa gu mdzad pa) (his Reverence) made several scratchings with his hand, scratched several times, at the door Mil. - 3. to exert one's self, to make every effort W., *srád-caṅ zúm-če* (srad cang zum ce) or táṅ-če* (tang ce) id. W.
C. *srab-ba-srib-be* (srab ba srib be) Ld., v. rab-rib (rab rib).
to rend, tear up, tear to pieces e.g. of a beast of prey: to tear up a person's body; *sral soṅ* (sral song) he has torn it to pieces C., W., hral-hrál (hral hral) Lex.
Cs.: Ssk. essence, substance; a mystical word.
W. *srig-la táṅ-če* (srig la tang ce), *srig-ga táṅ-če* (srig ga tang ce) to hang (a thief), *srig-la ši-če* (srig la shi ce) to hang one's self.

Cs., mig (mig) (or resp. spyan (spyan)) hrig-hrig byéd-pa (hrig hrig byed pa) or _dúg-pa ('dug pa), (= rig-ríg (rig rig)), to look this way and that, hither and thither Mil. nt.; hrig-ge-ba (hrig ge ba) looking in that manner Thgr.
S.g.; rná-ba hrid _byar (rna ba hrid 'byar) is explained by Wdn.: rná-ba lhág-pa p'yógs-su byar (rna ba lhag pa phyogs su byar) the ear leans feebly on the neck, as a sign of death.

1. round, globular, hril-hríl byéd-pa (hril hril byed pa) to writhe with pain, hril-hríl kar-kár byéd-pa (hril hril kar kar byed pa) to be writhing, and then again stretching one's self or starting up Pth. - 2. whole, tse hril-por (tshe hril por) for the whole life Glr., mgo hril-gyis gtúm-nas (mgo hril gyis gtum nas) wrapping up his whole head Glr. - 3. close, dense, hril _dús-te _dúg-pa (hril 'dus te 'dug pa) to sit or stand close together in rows Mil., C., W. Cf. ril-ba (ril ba).
adj. and abstract noun, rough, rugged; severe, austere; roughness etc.; hrúd-po (hrud po) adj. id. Cs.
to break, to smash Sch.

adj. and abstr. sbst. ragged, tattered; raggedness, hrul-bai dug-po (hrul ba'i dug po) a ragged coat Mil.; hrúl-po (hrul po) id., gos hrúl-po (gos hrul po) Dzl and elsewh. frq. - 2. sbst.: rags, tatters, gós-su hrúl-po gyón-pa (gos su hrul po gyon pa) to put on rags for a garment Pth.; hrúl-k'aṅ (hrul khang) a ruin, ruins Sch.
swollen Sch., hrem-mé (hrem me) id. Mng.

1. the first class of beings subject to metempsychosis, the gods, both those of Brahminical mythology, and the various national and local gods, with whom Buddhism came in contact. These local gods were incorporated into the system of Buddhism, when they were found to be too numerous and too much endeared to the people, to be entirely discarded and given up; so, most of them are worshipped even nowadays and presented with various offerings.
